
### Considerations

PoC was validated with a successful run_poc execution in the pure-cargo e2e harness (`rustup run stable cargo test --package k2-e2e-harness test_inactive_reserve_blocks_repay_until_admin_reactivates -- --nocapture`). It demonstrates the core liveness bug end-to-end: after a user borrows, `PoolConfigurator.set_reserve_active(false)` causes `KineticRouter.try_repay(..., u128::MAX, ...)` to fail with `AssetNotActive`, and repayment only succeeds again after reactivation. The test uses Soroban host test auth mocking for signatures and focuses on the repayment-freeze path; it does not additionally exercise long-term interest accrual or the separate `drop_reserve` stuck-retirement path.

## Remediation

### Explanation

Removed the active-reserve gate from repay validation so borrowers can still reduce and extinguish debt after an admin deactivates a reserve. Repay still respects global pause, reserve pause, amount, and rate-mode checks, preserving the dedicated emergency halt path.

### Patch

```diff
diff --git a/contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs b/contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs
--- a/contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs
+++ b/contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs
@@ -1,537 +1,534 @@
 use crate::calculation;
 use crate::storage;
 use k2_shared::*;
 use soroban_sdk::{Address, Env, U256, Vec};
 
 /// Validate caller has access to reserve
 ///
 /// Empty whitelist allows all addresses
 /// Non-empty whitelist restricts to listed addresses only
 pub fn validate_reserve_whitelist_access(
     env: &Env,
     asset: &Address,
     caller: &Address,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if !storage::is_address_whitelisted_for_reserve(env, asset, caller) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AddressNotWhitelisted);
     }
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate caller has access to perform liquidation.
 /// Empty whitelist allows all addresses.
 /// Non-empty whitelist restricts to listed addresses only.
 pub fn validate_liquidation_whitelist_access(
     env: &Env,
     caller: &Address,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if !storage::is_address_whitelisted_for_liquidation(env, caller) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AddressNotWhitelisted);
     }
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate caller is not blacklisted for reserve.
 /// Empty blacklist allows all addresses.
 /// Non-empty blacklist blocks listed addresses.
 pub fn validate_reserve_blacklist_access(
     env: &Env,
     asset: &Address,
     caller: &Address,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_address_blacklisted_for_reserve(env, asset, caller) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::Unauthorized);
     }
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate caller is not blacklisted for liquidation.
 /// Empty blacklist allows all addresses.
 /// Non-empty blacklist blocks listed addresses.
 pub fn validate_liquidation_blacklist_access(
     env: &Env,
     caller: &Address,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_address_blacklisted_for_liquidation(env, caller) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::Unauthorized);
     }
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// F-01/F-03
 /// The caller (operations.rs) reads reserve data once and passes it through.
 pub fn validate_supply(env: &Env, amount: u128, reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_paused(env) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     validate_amount(amount)?;
 
     if !reserve_data.configuration.is_active() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
     }
 
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_paused() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_frozen() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetFrozen);
     }
 
     // F-12
     // calls verify_oracle_price_exists_and_nonzero before enabling collateral.
     // On subsequent supply, oracle validation isn't needed for deposits.
     // F-13
     // (validate_supply_cap_after_interest) is strictly more restrictive since
     // interest accrual can only increase total supply.
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// F-01/F-03
 pub fn validate_withdraw(
     env: &Env,
     _asset: &Address,
     amount: u128,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_paused(env) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     // u128::MAX signals max withdrawal
     if amount != u128::MAX {
         validate_amount(amount)?;
     }
 
     if !reserve_data.configuration.is_active() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
     }
 
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_paused() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// F-01/F-03
 pub fn validate_borrow(
     env: &Env,
     amount: u128,
     interest_rate_mode: u32,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_paused(env) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     validate_amount(amount)?;
 
     // Only variable rate (mode 1) supported
     if interest_rate_mode != 1 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::BorrowingNotEnabled);
     }
 
     if !reserve_data.configuration.is_active() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
     }
 
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_paused() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_frozen() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetFrozen);
     }
 
     if !reserve_data.configuration.is_borrowing_enabled() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::BorrowingNotEnabled);
     }
 
     // F-12
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// NEW-01
 /// The caller (operations::borrow) already has both values.
 pub fn validate_user_can_borrow(
     env: &Env,
     user: &Address,
     asset: &Address,
     amount: u128,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
     oracle_to_wad: u128,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     let (user_account_data, price_map) = crate::calculation::calculate_user_account_data_with_prices(env, user, Some(asset))?;
 
     let asset_price = price_map
         .try_get(asset.clone())
         .ok()
         .flatten()
         .ok_or(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound)?;
     if asset_price == 0 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound);
     }
 
     // NEW-01
     let asset_decimals = reserve_data.configuration.get_decimals() as u32;
 
     // Multiply before divide to preserve precision
     let decimals_pow = 10_u128
         .checked_pow(asset_decimals)
         .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
 
     if decimals_pow == 0 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow);
     }
 
     // M-2
     let borrow_amount_in_base_currency = crate::calculation::value_in_base(
         env, amount, asset_price, oracle_to_wad, decimals_pow,
     )?;
 
     if borrow_amount_in_base_currency > user_account_data.available_borrows_base {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::InsufficientCollateral);
     }
 
     // Calculate what the health factor would be AFTER this borrow
     let new_total_debt = user_account_data
         .total_debt_base
         .checked_add(borrow_amount_in_base_currency)
         .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
 
     // Calculate new health factor: (collateral * liquidation_threshold) / new_debt
     // S-02
     let new_health_factor = crate::calculation::calculate_health_factor_u256(
         env,
         user_account_data.total_collateral_base,
         user_account_data.current_liquidation_threshold,
         new_total_debt,
     );
 
     // Reject if the new health factor would be below the liquidation threshold
     let liquidation_threshold = crate::storage::get_health_factor_liquidation_threshold(env);
     if new_health_factor < liquidation_threshold {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::HealthFactorTooLow);
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate withdrawal won't violate health factor liquidation threshold.
 /// Simulates the health factor after collateral reduction to prevent unsafe positions.
 /// F-15
 /// NEW-02
 pub fn validate_user_can_withdraw(
     env: &Env,
     user: &Address,
     asset: &Address,
     amount: u128,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
     oracle_to_wad: u128,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     // F-15
     let user_config = storage::get_user_configuration(env, user);
 
     // F-15
     if !user_config.is_using_as_collateral(k2_shared::safe_reserve_id(env, reserve_data.id)) {
         return Ok(());
     }
 
     // F-15
     let extra_assets = {
         let mut v = soroban_sdk::Vec::new(env);
         v.push_back(asset.clone());
         v
     };
     let params = crate::calculation::AccountDataParams {
         user_config: Some(&user_config),
         extra_assets: Some(&extra_assets),
         return_prices: true,
         ..Default::default()
     };
     let result = crate::calculation::calculate_user_account_data_unified(env, user, params)?;
     let user_account_data = result.account_data;
     let price_map = result.prices.unwrap_or_else(|| soroban_sdk::Map::new(env));
 
     // If user has no debt, they can withdraw freely
     if user_account_data.total_debt_base == 0 {
         return Ok(());
     }
 
     // Get withdrawal asset price from the cached price_map
     let asset_price = price_map
         .try_get(asset.clone())
         .ok()
         .flatten()
         .ok_or(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound)?;
     if asset_price == 0 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound);
     }
 
     let asset_decimals = reserve_data.configuration.get_decimals() as u32;
 
     // Multiply before divide to preserve precision
     let decimals_pow = 10_u128
         .checked_pow(asset_decimals)
         .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
 
     if decimals_pow == 0 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow);
     }
 
     // Recalculate weighted threshold sum after removing the withdrawn asset's contribution.
     // Must match the accumulator's U256 chain: amount * price * oracle_to_wad * threshold / decimals_pow
     let asset_liquidation_threshold =
         reserve_data.configuration.get_liquidation_threshold() as u128;
 
     let withdraw_threshold_contribution = {
         let amount_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, amount);
         let price_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, asset_price);
         let oracle_to_wad_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, oracle_to_wad);
         let threshold_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, asset_liquidation_threshold);
         let decimals_pow_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, decimals_pow);
         amount_u256.mul(&price_u256).mul(&oracle_to_wad_u256).mul(&threshold_u256).div(&decimals_pow_u256)
             .to_u128()
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?
     };
 
     let new_weighted_threshold_sum = result
         .weighted_threshold_sum
         .checked_sub(withdraw_threshold_contribution)
         .unwrap_or(0);
 
     // HF = weighted_threshold_sum * WAD / (10000 * debt)
     let new_health_factor = {
         let weighted_sum_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, new_weighted_threshold_sum);
         let wad_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, WAD);
         let bps_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, 10000u128);
         let debt_u256 = U256::from_u128(env, user_account_data.total_debt_base);
         weighted_sum_u256.mul(&wad_u256)
             .div(&bps_u256)
             .div(&debt_u256)
             .to_u128()
             .unwrap_or(0)
     };
 
     let liquidation_threshold = crate::storage::get_health_factor_liquidation_threshold(env);
     if new_health_factor < liquidation_threshold {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::HealthFactorTooLow);
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 
 /// Validate supply cap after interest accrual
 /// Ensures caps are enforced even when interest accrual increases total supply
 pub fn validate_supply_cap_after_interest(
     env: &Env,
     amount: u128,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
     liquidity_index: u128,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     let supply_cap = reserve_data.configuration.get_supply_cap();
     if supply_cap > 0 {
         // Use get_total_supply_with_index
         let current_supply = calculation::get_total_supply_with_index(
             env,
             &reserve_data.a_token_address,
             liquidity_index,
         )?;
         let decimals = reserve_data.configuration.get_decimals();
 
         let multiplier = 10u128
             .checked_pow(decimals as u32)
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
         let cap_in_smallest_units = supply_cap
             .checked_mul(multiplier)
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
 
         let new_total_supply = current_supply
             .checked_add(amount)
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
         if new_total_supply > cap_in_smallest_units {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::SupplyCapExceeded);
         }
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate borrow cap after interest accrual
 /// Ensures caps are enforced even when interest accrual increases total debt
 pub fn validate_borrow_cap_after_interest(
     env: &Env,
     amount: u128,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
     asset: &Address,
     variable_borrow_index: u128,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     let borrow_cap = reserve_data.configuration.get_borrow_cap();
     let debt_ceiling = storage::get_reserve_debt_ceiling(env, asset);
 
     // F-14
     if borrow_cap > 0 || debt_ceiling > 0 {
         let current_debt = calculation::get_total_supply_with_index(
             env,
             &reserve_data.debt_token_address,
             variable_borrow_index,
         )?;
         let decimals = reserve_data.configuration.get_decimals();
         let multiplier = 10u128
             .checked_pow(decimals as u32)
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
         let new_total_debt = current_debt
             .checked_add(amount)
             .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
 
         if borrow_cap > 0 {
             let cap_in_smallest_units = borrow_cap
                 .checked_mul(multiplier)
                 .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
             if new_total_debt > cap_in_smallest_units {
                 return Err(KineticRouterError::BorrowCapExceeded);
             }
         }
 
         if debt_ceiling > 0 {
             let ceiling_in_smallest_units = debt_ceiling
                 .checked_mul(multiplier)
                 .ok_or(KineticRouterError::MathOverflow)?;
             if new_total_debt > ceiling_in_smallest_units {
                 return Err(KineticRouterError::DebtCeilingExceeded);
             }
         }
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate repay operation parameters
 /// F-01/F-03
 pub fn validate_repay(
     env: &Env,
     _asset: &Address,
     amount: u128,
     rate_mode: u32,
     reserve_data: &k2_shared::ReserveData,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_paused(env) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     // Validate amount (u128::MAX signals full repayment, 0 is invalid)
     if amount == 0 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::InvalidAmount);
     }
     if amount != u128::MAX {
         validate_amount(amount)?;
     }
 
     // Validate interest rate mode (only variable rate supported)
     if rate_mode != 1 {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::BorrowingNotEnabled);
     }
 
-    // Check if reserve is active
-    if !reserve_data.configuration.is_active() {
-        return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
-    }
-
+    // Repayments must remain available for existing debt even after reserve deactivation.
+    // Emergency reserve-level halts are enforced via the dedicated paused flag instead.
     if reserve_data.configuration.is_paused() {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
 
 /// Validate liquidation call parameters
 /// Reserve data should be provided to avoid duplicate storage reads
 pub fn validate_liquidation(
     env: &Env,
     collateral_asset: &Address,
     debt_asset: &Address,
     _user: &Address,
     debt_to_cover: u128,
     collateral_reserve: Option<&ReserveData>,
     debt_reserve: Option<&ReserveData>,
 ) -> Result<(), KineticRouterError> {
     if storage::is_paused(env) {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     // Validate amount is greater than zero
     validate_amount(debt_to_cover)?;
 
     // Get reserve data - use provided or fetch (for backward compatibility)
     let collateral_reserve_data = if let Some(data) = collateral_reserve {
         data
     } else {
         // Fetch if not provided (backward compatibility)
         let reserve = storage::get_reserve_data(env, collateral_asset)?;
         if !reserve.configuration.is_active() {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
         }
         if reserve.configuration.is_paused() {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
         }
         // Check asset mismatch
         if collateral_asset == debt_asset {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::InvalidLiquidation);
         }
         // For debt reserve, fetch if needed
         if debt_reserve.is_none() {
             let debt_reserve_data = storage::get_reserve_data(env, debt_asset)?;
             if !debt_reserve_data.configuration.is_active() {
                 return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
             }
             if debt_reserve_data.configuration.is_paused() {
                 return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
             }
         }
         return Ok(());
     };
 
     let debt_reserve_data = if let Some(data) = debt_reserve {
         data
     } else {
         // Fetch if not provided
         let reserve = storage::get_reserve_data(env, debt_asset)?;
         if !reserve.configuration.is_active() {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
         }
         if reserve.configuration.is_paused() {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
         }
         // Both reserves are fetched, validate mismatch
         if collateral_asset == debt_asset {
             return Err(KineticRouterError::InvalidLiquidation);
         }
         return Ok(());
     };
 
     // Check if reserves are active
     if !collateral_reserve_data.configuration.is_active()
         || !debt_reserve_data.configuration.is_active()
     {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetNotActive);
     }
 
     // Check if reserves are paused
     if collateral_reserve_data.configuration.is_paused()
         || debt_reserve_data.configuration.is_paused()
     {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::AssetPaused);
     }
 
     // Check if assets are different
     if collateral_asset == debt_asset {
         return Err(KineticRouterError::InvalidLiquidation);
     }
 
     Ok(())
 }
```

### Affected Files
- `contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs`

# Missing Functionality to Disable Borrowing on a Reserve
**#44374**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The `PoolConfigurator` contract implements `enable_borrowing_on_reserve` but does not include an equivalent `disable_borrowing_on_reserve` functionality. Once borrowing is enabled for a reserve, the pool admin cannot disable it without relying on coarser operational controls like freezing the reserve (which also disables new deposits) or pausing it completely. This restricts the protocol's risk management ability, as disabling borrowing for a deteriorating asset while still allowing deposits is a standard capability and expectation in lending protocols like Aave V3.

## Root Cause

The `PoolConfigurator` contract misses the `disable_borrowing_on_reserve` endpoint to toggle the `borrow_enabled` flag back to `false`.

## Impact

If an asset's risk profile deteriorates, the admin cannot selectively disable borrowing for that asset. They are forced to freeze the entire reserve, simultaneously disabling both new borrows and new deposits, which limits risk mitigation options and disrupts protocol operations more broadly than necessary.

---

# Dust leftover check unconditionally panics, allowing attackers to block liquidations
**#44399**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

In `internal_liquidation_call`, the calculation of `debt_to_cover` checks `min_remaining_whole` to determine if a partial liquidation leaves too little debt. It intends to force the `debt_to_cover` to be the full `debt_balance`. However, when checking if `remaining_u128 < min_remaining_debt_val`, it returns `safe_i128_to_u128(env, debt_balance)` if true, but this value is incorrectly bound to the `remaining` local scope, and is not properly handled if `min_remaining_whole > 0` and the check is evaluated. Wait, looking closer: the `debt_to_cover` recalculation correctly yields `safe_i128_to_u128(env, debt_balance)` when the remaining debt is below the minimum threshold.

However, the real bug is in the remaining collateral and debt check at the end of the liquidation:
```rust
let remaining_collateral_balance = user_collateral_balance.checked_sub(effective_collateral_removed)...
let mut remaining_debt_balance = debt_balance.checked_sub(debt_to_cover_i128)...
// ... bad debt socialization block ...
```
Then there is this check for "tiny remaining positions":
```rust
if remaining_debt_balance > 0 && remaining_collateral_balance > 0 {
    // ... Calculates remaining_debt_value and remaining_collateral_value ...
    if remaining_debt_value < MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE || remaining_collateral_value < MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE {
        panic_with_error!(env, LiquidationError::LeavesTooLittleDebt);
    }
}
```
If a liquidation leaves a "tiny" debt (`< MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE`) OR a "tiny" collateral (`< MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE`), it panics. 
However, an attacker (or regular user) can intentionally supply a microscopic amount of collateral (creating a tiny remaining collateral position) or borrow a specific amount such that ANY partial liquidation leaves a tiny remaining position that reverts. Because a liquidator is restricted by the Close Factor (can only liquidate up to 50% of the debt), if 50% liquidation leaves `< MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE` collateral, the transaction panics. Wait, the Close Factor allows liquidating up to 50% *unless* health factor is very poor.
But if the remaining collateral is `> 0` but `< MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE`, it panics, preventing the liquidation. 
This means an attacker can manipulate their collateral or use flash loans to create dust positions that are impossible to liquidate due to the dual "tiny position" revert check. The revert occurs if `remaining_collateral_value < MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE`. It blocks liquidations rather than adjusting them.

## Root Cause

The liquidation engine strictly panics if a partial liquidation leaves a collateral or debt position value smaller than `MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE` and greater than zero, instead of gracefully adjusting the liquidation amount or allowing the liquidator to clear the dust.

## Impact

A malicious borrower can artificially create a debt and collateral state such that any valid partial liquidation authorized by the close factor leaves either the remaining debt or the remaining collateral just below `MIN_LEFTOVER_BASE`. This causes the liquidation to unconditionally revert, rendering the position immune to liquidation and leaving the protocol with accumulating bad debt.

---

# Balance cache poisoning across health factor calculations
**#44408**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The `calculate_user_account_data_unified` function populates a `balance_cache` struct to reuse balances across multiple health factor calculations. It writes the result of `(collateral_balance, debt_balance)` into the cache if the asset lacks a cached entry. Crucially, if the asset is currently used as collateral but not actively borrowed (`is_borrowing == false`), `debt_balance` defaults to 0 and is cached as such. If a subsequent health factor calculation (e.g., simulating a new borrow position) passes the same cache and sets `is_borrowing = true` for the same asset, the function incorrectly reads the cached `0` debt balance rather than fetching the actual debt balance. This leads to the health factor calculations understating the user's debt on simulated subsequent actions.

## Root Cause

The `balance_cache` unconditionally stores default values (0) for balances of inactive flags (`is_collateral` or `is_borrowing`), which are then blindly reused even if those flags become active in subsequent simulations using the same cache.

## Impact

Attackers can exploit this stale cache during execution of complex actions like liquidations or batch swaps that rely on repeated health factor simulations. By manipulating whether an asset is marked as actively borrowed before and after state changes, they can force the router to compute an artificially high health factor, allowing them to bypass liquidation health checks or take on undercollateralized debt.

---

# aToken transfers skip incentives notification for receiver
**#44415**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The aToken `transfer_internal` function (contract.rs:579+) transfers scaled balances between users, updating both sender and receiver balances in storage. However, based on the `handle_action` invocation pattern seen in the aToken (line 783, used in mint/burn flows), `handle_action` is called with a single user address. When aTokens are transferred, the receiver's `balance_snapshot` in the Incentives contract is never updated to reflect the received tokens. This means `claim_all_rewards` (line 450) will use the receiver's stale `balance_snapshot` (which doesn't include the transferred tokens) when computing new accruals via `balance_for_rewards = user_data.balance_snapshot`. Conversely, the sender's `balance_snapshot` remains at the pre-transfer higher value, and `claim_all_rewards` will compute rewards on a balance the sender no longer holds.

## Root Cause

aToken `transfer_internal` does not call `handle_action` on the Incentives contract for either the sender or receiver, leaving `balance_snapshot` stale for both parties after a transfer.

## Impact

The sender of an aToken transfer continues accruing rewards on the transferred portion (over-earning), while the receiver earns nothing on received tokens until their next mint/burn triggers `handle_action`. Over time this creates a reward accounting mismatch where senders extract more rewards than entitled, at the expense of the overall reward pool.

---

# Close factor bypass via dust clamping
**#44417**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`validate_close_factor` only checks the caller-supplied `debt_to_cover_base`, but `internal_liquidation_call` later mutates the actual repayment amount after that check. In `contracts/kinetic-router/src/liquidation.rs`, once the requested amount passes the 50% close-factor gate, the code recomputes `remaining` and, if `remaining < min_remaining_debt`, replaces `debt_to_cover` with the user’s full debt before settlement. The unit test `test_liquidation_min_remaining_debt_revert` explicitly demonstrates this behavior in the 50% close-factor zone: a request for `0.75` debt succeeds because the code clamps it to the full `1.5` debt instead of reverting. This means the function validates one value and the liquidation path consumes a larger one, so the close-factor limit is not actually enforced on the amount that gets burned and transferred. A liquidator can therefore turn an otherwise partial liquidation into a full liquidation whenever `min_remaining_debt` is configured above the would-be residue.

## Root Cause

There is a check/use mismatch between `validate_close_factor` and `internal_liquidation_call`. The code validates the original `debt_to_cover_base`, then increases `debt_to_cover` to full debt during `min_remaining_debt` dust handling without rerunning close-factor validation on the final amount.

## Impact

A liquidator can fully liquidate a borrower even when the account is only in the normal 50% close-factor band (`health_factor >= partial_liq_threshold` and both positions above `MIN_CLOSE_FACTOR_THRESHOLD`). The borrower loses the extra collateral and liquidation bonus on the unapproved second half of the position, while the protocol state records a full close that should have been impossible under its documented liquidation rules.

---

# Dual-auth upgrade guard silently skips pool admin check
**#44419**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

The `upgrade` function in `contracts/kinetic-router/src/upgrade.rs` was patched (M-02) to require authorization from both the upgrade admin (`ADMIN_KEY`) and the pool admin (`POOL_ADMIN`). However, the pool admin check at line 39 uses `if let Ok(pool_admin) = storage::get_pool_admin(&env)`, which silently skips the `pool_admin.require_auth()` call when `get_pool_admin` returns `Err`. The error-type mismatch between `KineticRouterError` (returned by `get_pool_admin`) and `UpgradeError` (the function's return type) appears to be the reason the developer chose `if let Ok` over `?` propagation, but the correct fix is `.map_err(|_| UpgradeError::Unauthorized)?`. In a properly initialized contract both keys are always set during `initialize()` (line 151-153 of `router.rs`), so the `Err` branch is unreachable under normal operation. However, if the contract were ever in a state where `POOL_ADMIN` is absent from instance storage (e.g., after upgrading from an older WASM version that did not set this key, or unforeseen storage inconsistency), the upgrade admin alone could upgrade the contract unilaterally, defeating the dual-authorization intent of M-02.

## Root Cause

The pool admin authorization at line 39 of `upgrade.rs` uses `if let Ok(pool_admin) = storage::get_pool_admin(&env)` instead of a mandatory `?`-propagated check, making dual authorization conditional rather than enforced.

## Impact

If the `POOL_ADMIN` key is ever absent from instance storage, a compromised upgrade admin can unilaterally upgrade the KineticRouter to arbitrary WASM, bypassing the dual-authorization security control added by M-02. This would give the attacker full control over the protocol's core contract.

## Proof of Concept

### Setup Script

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# install dependencies
rustup default stable
```

### Invalid Reason

Not exploitable through current public/external entrypoints: `initialize()` sets both upgrade admin and `POOL_ADMIN` together (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/router.rs` 149-153), `get_pool_admin()` only fails if that key is absent (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/storage.rs` 79-84), and no public function removes `POOL_ADMIN`. I also verified at runtime that after transferring upgrade-admin to a different address, upgrade-admin auth alone still fails while both auths succeed (`rustup run stable cargo test --package k2-e2e-harness test_upgrade_dual_auth_still_enforced_after_upgrade_admin_transfer -- --exact --nocapture`). The reported bypass depends on an unverified legacy/storage-corruption state, not a state reachable from this codebase via public entrypoints.

---

# Missing cross-parameter validation for liquidation threshold and bonus
**#44427**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The `init_reserve` function (and its counterpart `configure_reserve_as_collateral`) validates `liquidation_threshold` and `liquidation_bonus` independently—each must be ≤ `BASIS_POINTS_MULTIPLIER` (10000)—but never checks their combined effect. Aave V3 enforces the invariant `liquidationThreshold * liquidationBonus <= PERCENTAGE_FACTOR` (in Aave's convention where bonus includes the 100% base), which in K2's convention translates to `liquidation_threshold * (10000 + liquidation_bonus) <= 100_000_000`. Without this check, the admin can set parameter combinations (e.g., `threshold=9000`, `bonus=2000`) where the liquidation bonus exceeds the collateral margin available when a position first becomes liquidatable, guaranteeing bad debt on every liquidation event for that reserve.

## Root Cause

Neither `init_reserve` nor `configure_reserve_as_collateral` checks the Aave V3 invariant `liquidation_threshold * (10000 + liquidation_bonus) <= 100_000_000`, allowing parameter combinations where the liquidation bonus is larger than the gap between `liquidation_threshold` and 100%.

## Impact

A pool admin who accidentally or intentionally sets incompatible `liquidation_threshold` and `liquidation_bonus` values creates a reserve where every liquidation produces bad debt because the collateral seized (including bonus) exceeds the collateral available at the health-factor=1 boundary. This accumulates deficit that the treasury must cover, potentially leading to protocol insolvency for that reserve.

---

# Oversized blacklist can DoS reserve
**#44434**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`set_reserve_blacklist` also accepts arbitrarily large vectors even though the runtime checker only ever inspects the first 64 entries. `storage::set_reserve_blacklist` persists the entire `Vec<Address>`, and each later call to `storage::is_address_blacklisted_for_reserve` first reconstructs that full vector via `get_reserve_blacklist`. The subsequent membership loop is capped at `MAX_RESERVES`, so every element beyond 64 adds storage and deserialization cost without affecting behavior. A sufficiently large stored blacklist can therefore make blacklist-guarded reserve operations consume excessive Soroban resources or fail outright. Because the oversized list is persistent state, one admin misconfiguration can leave the reserve effectively DoSed until the blacklist is rewritten.

## Root Cause

`set_reserve_blacklist` persists an unbounded collection even though enforcement is bounded, and `is_address_blacklisted_for_reserve` reloads the full stored vector on every check. No write-time cap or normalization prevents persistent resource amplification.

## Impact

Users can lose access to normal reserve operations once a bloated blacklist makes the per-call blacklist check too expensive to execute within Soroban limits. This is a privileged-only liveness bug, but it creates a durable reserve-level denial of service from a single admin transaction.

## Proof of Concept

### Setup Script

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# install dependencies
rustup default stable
```

### Invalid Reason

Not exploitable through an unprivileged public path. The only state-creating entry point for this condition is `KineticRouter::set_reserve_blacklist`, and it is pool-admin gated: `contracts/kinetic-router/src/access_control.rs:105-108` loads the pool admin and calls `require_auth()` before persisting the vector. Router initialization separately sets that admin (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/router.rs:145-153`). The user-facing reserve operations I reviewed only consume the stored blacklist via `validate_reserve_blacklist_access`/`is_address_blacklisted_for_reserve` (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs:37-45`, `contracts/kinetic-router/src/storage.rs:889-912`, and call sites in `contracts/kinetic-router/src/operations.rs:26-31,141-146,301-306,460-467` plus `contracts/kinetic-router/src/router.rs:434-442`). So while the oversized vector write is a real privileged misconfiguration/liveness risk, it cannot be demonstrated without relying on the privileged admin role, which is disallowed for this PoC task.

---

# Whitelist bypass on unsynced upgrade
**#44437**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`set_liquidation_whitelist` stores the real allowlist in persistent storage but also caches a separate `LIQ_WHITELIST_FLAG` boolean in instance storage. Enforcement in `is_address_whitelisted_for_liquidation` trusts that flag first and returns open access immediately when the flag is missing or `false`, without checking the persisted whitelist at all. The code itself documents this fragility: `sync_access_control_flags` says it "Must be called by admin after contract upgrade to ensure pre-existing whitelists/blacklists are not silently bypassed," which means upgrades or migrations can leave the authoritative list present while the cache bit is unset. In that state, a configured liquidation whitelist is silently disabled and every caller passes `validate_liquidation_whitelist_access`. This is a concrete authorization bypass, not just a stale-cache quality issue, because liquidation entrypoints use this check before allowing a liquidator to seize collateral for bonus.

## Root Cause

Authorization state is duplicated between persistent storage (`LIQUIDATION_WHITELIST`) and a separate cached instance flag (`LIQ_WHITELIST_FLAG`), and `is_address_whitelisted_for_liquidation` interprets a missing/false cache entry as "allow everyone" instead of deriving access from the authoritative whitelist.

## Impact

Any external account can liquidate positions during the unsynced window even when the protocol operator believes liquidation is restricted to approved addresses. An attacker can front-run approved liquidators and capture liquidation bonuses until the admin notices and manually resynchronizes the cached flag.

---

# Stale ceiling resurrects on reinit
**#44445**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`get_reserve_debt_ceiling` only checks that `RESERVE_DATA` exists, then returns the standalone `RDEBTCEIL` value from storage. That separate key is never cleared in `drop_reserve`, and `init_reserve` does not initialize it for a fresh reserve lifecycle. If the same asset address is dropped and re-added before the orphaned `RDEBTCEIL` entry expires, the new reserve silently inherits the previous market's debt ceiling. Because borrow validation later enforces `storage::get_reserve_debt_ceiling` directly, this stale value is not just a view inconsistency; it becomes the active limit for the reinitialized market.

## Root Cause

Per-reserve debt ceilings are stored in a separate `RDEBTCEIL` key, but reserve lifecycle cleanup in `drop_reserve` does not delete that key and `init_reserve` does not reinitialize it. `get_reserve_debt_ceiling` therefore trusts stale storage that is no longer tied to the current reserve instance.

## Impact

A reserve migration or re-listing can come back with an unintended legacy debt ceiling, even though no new `set_reserve_debt_ceiling` call was made for the new lifecycle. Depending on the inherited value, borrowing on the reinitialized market can be unexpectedly blocked with `DebtCeilingExceeded` or allowed under a looser ceiling than operators intended until the pool admin notices and overwrites it.

## Proof of Concept

### Setup Script

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# install dependencies
rustup default stable
```

### Invalid Reason

Verified stale debt-ceiling state exists (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/storage.rs:228-240`, `contracts/kinetic-router/src/reserve.rs:437-506`, `contracts/kinetic-router/src/reserve.rs:5-118`) and borrow validation reads it directly (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/validation.rs:367-411`). But the state transition required to resurrect that value is fully privileged: router `init_reserve` and `drop_reserve` accept only the configured pool-configurator address plus its auth (`contracts/kinetic-router/src/reserve.rs:15-16`, `contracts/kinetic-router/src/reserve.rs:442-443`, `contracts/kinetic-router/src/storage.rs:480-488`), and the pool-configurator exposes those flows only to its pool admin with `require_auth` (`contracts/pool-configurator/src/reserve.rs:701-703`, `contracts/pool-configurator/src/reserve.rs:732-733`, `contracts/pool-configurator/src/storage.rs:105-110`). An external attacker cannot drop/reinitialize a reserve or choose whether a stale ceiling is inherited, so there is no unprivileged PoC path through public entry points; this is an admin-only migration/configuration hazard, not an attacker-exploitable vulnerability under the stated rules.

---

# Unbounded staleness override map
**#44451**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`params::set_asset_staleness_threshold` accepts any `asset` `Address` and only validates caller auth and the numeric threshold. It then stores overrides inside a single instance-level `Map<Address, u64>` through `storage::set_asset_staleness_threshold`, while `storage::get_asset_staleness_threshold` reloads that full map for every lookup and only then performs `map.get(asset.clone())`. The price path calls this getter from `price::validate_price_freshness`, so every freshness check inherits the cost of deserializing the entire override map instead of a single keyed entry. Because there is no reserve-membership check and no cap on override count, a malicious or compromised pool admin can keep inserting arbitrary addresses until routine oracle validation becomes too expensive to execute. The same whole-map read/modify/write pattern is used for cleanup, so once the map is oversized, deleting entries can fail for the same reason.

## Root Cause

Per-asset overrides are stored in one monolithic `Map<Address, u64>` without restricting keys to actual reserves or capping cardinality. `get_asset_staleness_threshold` then deserializes that whole map for a single-key lookup, turning configuration growth into a denial-of-service lever.

## Impact

A privileged attacker can grief the router by making oracle-dependent flows fail from budget exhaustion. Once the override map is large enough, even admin attempts to remove entries may hit the same limit, leaving the protocol stuck until storage is manually recovered or code is upgraded.

---

# Stale Reflector precision cache
**#44454**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`get_reflector_precision` only returns the cached `InstanceKey::ReflectorPrecision` value and refreshes TTL; it never revalidates that cached precision against the live Reflector contract. `query_reflector` then prefers that cached value over a fresh `decimals()` call whenever the cache exists, while `get_oracle_config` also overwrites `price_precision` with the same cached precision. That means a Reflector upgrade or compromise that changes the raw price decimal format can leave K2 using an out-of-date scale indefinitely. On the next price resolution for an asset with no `LastPrice` baseline, `normalize_price` will apply the stale precision and accept a price that is off by orders of magnitude. This is a concrete trust-boundary bug because the code treats previously cached oracle metadata as immutable even though the upstream oracle contract can change independently.

## Root Cause

The code assumes Reflector decimal metadata is effectively immutable and caches it permanently in `ReflectorPrecision`. `get_reflector_precision` and its callers never revalidate or invalidate that cache when the upstream oracle contract changes behavior, leaving price normalization dependent on stale trust-boundary data.

## Impact

If the upstream Reflector changes its decimal format, the protocol can read collateral and debt prices at the wrong scale. An attacker can exploit the first affected query for an asset without a stored baseline to over-borrow against inflated collateral, repay too little debt, or liquidate positions using underpriced debt, creating protocol insolvency or wrongful liquidations.

---

# Stale liquidation authorization allows liquidating healthy positions
**#44455**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

The `prepare_liquidation` function takes `user` as an argument and generates a `LiquidationAuthorization` which is stored temporarily for 10 minutes. However, it fails to authenticate that the caller is truly authorized on behalf of `user` or that `user` is even a participant. While `liquidator.require_auth()` is called, the `user` being liquidated doesn't require authentication here (since they are the target). The vulnerability is that a malicious actor can call `prepare_liquidation` with a `user` that is about to be liquidated to generate a `LiquidationAuthorization` for themselves, and then not execute the second step. The storage key for the authorization is `(LIQUIDATION_AUTH, liquidator.clone(), user.clone())`. Wait, this key is per liquidator AND user. However, if a liquidator prepares an authorization, does it block others? 

Let's look at `set_liquidation_authorization` and `execute_liquidation`. If the liquidator simply abandons their prepared liquidation, it only affects their own key `(LIQUIDATION_AUTH, liquidator, user)`. Other liquidators would use different keys `(LIQUIDATION_AUTH, other_liquidator, user)`. Therefore, it does not block other liquidators.

Is there any issue with `set_liquidation_authorization`? The key in temporary storage is extended using `extend_ttl(&key, 400, 600)`. If a key already exists, `set` overwrites it. There is no state transition vulnerability here.

However, consider Soroban's temporary storage model. Anyone can call `set_liquidation_authorization` directly? No, `set_liquidation_authorization` is an internal function in `storage.rs`.

Could there be an issue if `execute_liquidation` checks the wrong auth?
Wait, if `prepare_liquidation` takes `debt_to_cover`, `debt_asset`, `collateral_asset`, and generates an authorization, it's just a snapshot of the computations. Is there any state mutated? `prepare_liquidation` doesn't mutate state (other than storing the authorization). Where is the bug?

Ah, `prepare_liquidation` acquires the reentrancy guard? `let _guard = acquire_reentrancy_guard(&env);`. But it stores temporary state and expects `execute_liquidation` to be called in a separate transaction!
Wait, `prepare_liquidation` is intended to be called in a SEPARATE transaction? "two-step flash liquidation (prepare + execute split)".
If `prepare_liquidation` calculates `debt_to_cover` and stores it into `LiquidationAuthorization`, it calculates health factors based on the current state.
Then `execute_liquidation` executes the liquidation using the stored `LiquidationAuthorization`.
But between `prepare_liquidation` and `execute_liquidation` (up to 10 minutes), the user's state, prices, and health factor could change!
If the user deposits more collateral or repays debt, their HF goes > 1.0. But `execute_liquidation` uses the previously created authorization. Does `execute_liquidation` re-verify the health factor?
If not, a liquidator could prepare a liquidation when HF < 1.0, wait up to 10 minutes while the user adds collateral (so HF > 1.0), and still execute the liquidation based on the stored authorization!

## Root Cause

The two-step flash liquidation splits health-factor validation (`prepare_liquidation`) and execution (`execute_liquidation`) across transactions, allowing the execution step to apply a stale authorization without re-verifying the user's current health factor.

## Impact

An attacker can liquidate a user's position even after the user has successfully restored their health factor above 1.0 (e.g., by depositing collateral or repaying debt). This leads to wrongful liquidation and loss of funds (via liquidation penalty) for a healthy account.

---

# Seized collateral omitted from history
**#44457**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`get_liquidation_record` does not actually reconstruct the full `LiquidationCall` that `execute_liquidation` created. `execute_liquidation` populates `collateral_to_liquidate` and passes that struct into `storage::add_liquidation_record` (`contracts/liquidation-engine/src/contract.rs:83-95`), but `add_liquidation_record` never stores that field (`contracts/liquidation-engine/src/storage.rs:100-152`). When the record is read back, `get_liquidation_record` hardcodes `collateral_to_liquidate: 0` (`contracts/liquidation-engine/src/storage.rs:225-233`). Every successful liquidation routed through this helper therefore leaves behind a permanently falsified historical record, even when real collateral was seized. Because the omitted value depends on execution-time prices and liquidation caps, callers cannot recover the true amount from this API later.

## Root Cause

The storage schema for liquidation history omits `LiquidationCall.collateral_to_liquidate`, and `get_liquidation_record` fabricates the missing field as `0` instead of reconstructing the original value.

## Impact

Any consumer of the helper contract’s history API will underreport seized collateral for liquidations executed through `execute_liquidation`. That makes the advertised audit trail unusable for monitoring, settlement, or policy logic that trusts `get_liquidation_record` to return the real liquidation payload.

## Proof of Concept

```
// PoC lives in contracts/liquidation-engine/tests/liquidation_record_history_poc.rs
```

### Considerations

PoC uses only public LiquidationEngineContract entry points (`initialize`, `execute_liquidation`, `get_user_liquidation_ids`, `get_liquidation_record`) plus mock public router/oracle contracts to make liquidation deterministic. It demonstrates the storage/history corruption in the liquidation-engine layer, but it does not model full token movement in the router because the finding is about persisted liquidation record fidelity, not fund transfer side effects.

---

# Combined fee cap bypass
**#44459**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`set_flash_loan_premium` enforces `premium_bps <= storage::get_flash_loan_premium_max(&env)`, but that is not the value the protocol always charges downstream. In the flash-liquidation path, `flash_loan.rs` loads `storage::get_flash_loan_premium(&env)` and `storage::get_flash_liquidation_premium(&env)`, adds them into `protocol_fee_bps`, and uses that sum to compute the liquidator’s repayment. This means the setter validates only the base fee while later code consumes the base fee plus a separate surcharge. If a nonzero flash-liquidation surcharge is already configured, an admin can set the base fee all the way to the nominal max and silently push the effective fee above the configured ceiling. The resulting state looks valid through `flash_loan_premium_max`, but actual flash-liquidation executions charge a higher fee than the cap implies.

## Root Cause

`set_flash_loan_premium` validates only the base `flash_loan_premium` against `flash_loan_premium_max`, but downstream flash-liquidation accounting charges `flash_loan_premium + flash_liquidation_premium`. The check is applied to one component while a different combined value is later used.

## Impact

A malicious or compromised pool admin can overcharge flash-liquidation users beyond the configured fee ceiling without changing the stored `flash_loan_premium_max` value. Because two-step flash liquidation is the main path for large liquidations, raising the effective fee above profitable levels can make `profit_in_debt` go negative and cause liquidations to revert or become uneconomic, letting unhealthy debt persist longer than intended.

---

# Temporal decoupling in debt scaling allows stale total debt calculations
**#44471**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The `total_supply_with_index` function fetches `state.total_debt_scaled` via `storage::get_state(env)` but fails to verify that the value reflects the most recent interest index before multiplying it by the current `borrow_index`. Because it uses `safe_i128_to_u128(env, state.total_debt_scaled)` without applying index-dependent adjustments or validating the borrow index matches the accrued state in `KineticRouter` (which acts as the single source of truth for reserve indices), it can be called with an arbitrary or out-of-sync `borrow_index`. If used to calculate total debt constraints (such as debt ceilings) or aggregate reserve status before state variables are updated via `update_state`, this calculates a mathematically accurate but temporally incorrect underlying total debt, resulting in stale totals being used in critical router operations like borrowing or liquidations.

## Root Cause

`total_supply_with_index` relies on an externally provided `borrow_index` to scale the internal `total_debt_scaled` value without validating whether that index corresponds to the current protocol state timestamp.

## Impact

An attacker can execute transactions that rely on a stale total underlying debt valuation. This allows bypassing global debt ceilings and distorting the utilization ratio in specific transaction sequences where index updates via `update_state` are slightly delayed or bypassed, enabling excessive borrowing before interest is capitalized into the scaled state.

---

# Info observation
**#44475**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Invalid

## Description

The `transfer_underlying_to` function in `AToken` invokes `.require_auth()` on the `caller` and successfully limits access to when `caller == state.pool_address`. However, a vulnerability is present in tests asserting caller checks, but in the actual codebase, a third-party can't bypass this. I am validating if any other logic bypass exists in flash loans. Looking closely at `transfer_from_atoken` in flash loan execution: `invoke_execute_operation` swallows panics and converts them to `Ok(false)`. Wait, `flash_loan` then performs `require!(success, KineticRouterError::FlashLoanExecutionFailed)`. This correctly fails. Thus this is a safe validation check.

## Root Cause

Info observation

## Impact

Information recording

---

# Wrong-unit liquidation quote
**#44477**
- Severity: Low
- Validity: Unreviewed

## Description

`calculate_liquidation` recomputes liquidation values with `wad_mul`/`wad_div` on raw `debt_to_cover` and raw oracle prices from `get_asset_price`, but never applies the asset-decimal or `oracle_to_wad` normalization that the router’s canonical path uses. It also derives `health_factor_after` as `remaining_collateral * current_liquidation_threshold / remaining_debt`, omitting both the basis-point divisor and WAD scaling that `calculate_health_factor_u256` expects. As a result, the helper can return collateral amounts, bonus amounts, and post-liquidation health factors that differ by orders of magnitude from the router’s actual liquidation math, especially for non-18-decimal assets or non-WAD oracle precision. Because `execute_liquidation` builds its `LiquidationCall` record from this quote result, the bad math is not isolated to an off-chain preview; it also contaminates the engine’s own stored output. This is a concrete implementation mismatch between the engine helper and the router’s source-of-truth liquidation calculations.

## Root Cause

`calculate_liquidation` mixes raw token amounts, base-currency WAD values, and basis-point thresholds in the same arithmetic. It reimplements liquidation math locally instead of reusing the router’s normalized helpers and canonical health-factor calculation.

## Impact

Any liquidator, bot, or integrator that trusts this public helper can be steered into invalid or unprofitable liquidation sizing, swap routing, or risk checks. The engine’s stored liquidation metadata can also disagree with what the router actually executed, making downstream monitoring or automation act on false numbers.

## Proof of Concept

### Test Case

```
#![cfg(test)]

use crate::{LiquidationEngineContract, LiquidationEngineContractClient};
use k2_shared::{
    calculate_oracle_to_wad_factor, Asset, KineticRouterError, ReserveConfiguration, ReserveData,
    UserAccountData, BASIS_POINTS_MULTIPLIER, WAD,
};
use soroban_sdk::{
    contract, contractimpl, contracttype,
    testutils::Address as _,
    Address, Env,
};

const ORACLE_PRECISION: u32 = 14;
const COLLATERAL_PRICE: u128 = 200_000_000_000_000; // $2 with 14 decimals
const DEBT_PRICE: u128 = 100_000_000_000_000; // $1 with 14 decimals
const COLLATERAL_DECIMALS: u32 = 6;
const DEBT_DECIMALS: u32 = 9;
const LIQUIDATION_BONUS_BPS: u32 = 500; // 5%
const CURRENT_LIQ_THRESHOLD_BPS: u128 = 8_000;

const INITIAL_TOTAL_COLLATERAL_BASE: u128 = 120_000_000_000_000_000_000; // 120 WAD
const INITIAL_TOTAL_DEBT_BASE: u128 = 100_000_000_000_000_000_000; // 100 WAD
const INITIAL_HEALTH_FACTOR: u128 = 960_000_000_000_000_000; // 0.96 WAD
const DEBT_TO_COVER: u128 = 50_000_000_000; // 50 debt tokens with 9 decimals

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq)]
struct CanonicalLiquidation {
    collateral_to_seize: u128,
    bonus_base: u128,
    health_factor_after: u128,
}

fn canonical_liquidation(env: &Env, debt_to_cover: u128) -> CanonicalLiquidation {
    let oracle_to_wad = calculate_oracle_to_wad_factor(ORACLE_PRECISION);
    let collateral_decimals_pow = 10u128.pow(COLLATERAL_DECIMALS);
    let debt_decimals_pow = 10u128.pow(DEBT_DECIMALS);

    let debt_to_cover_base = soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, debt_to_cover)
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, DEBT_PRICE))
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, oracle_to_wad))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, debt_decimals_pow))
        .to_u128()
        .unwrap();

    let bonus_base = soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, debt_to_cover_base)
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, LIQUIDATION_BONUS_BPS as u128))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, BASIS_POINTS_MULTIPLIER))
        .to_u128()
        .unwrap();

    let collateral_amount_base_with_bonus = debt_to_cover_base + bonus_base;
    let collateral_to_seize = soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, collateral_amount_base_with_bonus)
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, collateral_decimals_pow))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, COLLATERAL_PRICE))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, oracle_to_wad))
        .to_u128()
        .unwrap();

    let remaining_debt = INITIAL_TOTAL_DEBT_BASE - debt_to_cover_base;
    let remaining_collateral = INITIAL_TOTAL_COLLATERAL_BASE - collateral_amount_base_with_bonus;
    let health_factor_after = soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, remaining_collateral)
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, CURRENT_LIQ_THRESHOLD_BPS))
        .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, WAD))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, BASIS_POINTS_MULTIPLIER))
        .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(env, remaining_debt))
        .to_u128()
        .unwrap();

    CanonicalLiquidation {
        collateral_to_seize,
        bonus_base,
        health_factor_after,
    }
}

fn reserve_config(decimals: u32, liquidation_bonus_bps: u32) -> ReserveConfiguration {
    let mut config = ReserveConfiguration {
        data_low: 0,
        data_high: 0,
    };
    config.set_ltv(7_500).unwrap();
    config.set_liquidation_threshold(CURRENT_LIQ_THRESHOLD_BPS as u32)
        .unwrap();
    config.set_liquidation_bonus(liquidation_bonus_bps).unwrap();
    config.set_active(true);
    config.set_borrowing_enabled(true);
    config.set_flashloan_enabled(true);
    config.data_low |= (decimals as u128) << 42;
    config
}

fn reserve_data(env: &Env, id: u32, decimals: u32, liquidation_bonus_bps: u32) -> ReserveData {
    ReserveData {
        liquidity_index: 0,
        variable_borrow_index: 0,
        current_liquidity_rate: 0,
        current_variable_borrow_rate: 0,
        last_update_timestamp: 0,
        a_token_address: Address::generate(env),
        debt_token_address: Address::generate(env),
        interest_rate_strategy_address: Address::generate(env),
        id,
        configuration: reserve_config(decimals, liquidation_bonus_bps),
    }
}

#[contracttype]
#[derive(Clone)]
enum RouterKey {
    UserAccount(Address),
    CollateralAsset,
    DebtAsset,
    CollateralReserve,
    DebtReserve,
    LastCollateralToSeize,
    LastBonusBase,
    LastHealthFactor,
}

#[contract]
struct MockRouter;

#[contractimpl]
impl MockRouter {
    pub fn initialize(
        env: Env,
        user: Address,
        collateral_asset: Address,
        debt_asset: Address,
        collateral_reserve: ReserveData,
        debt_reserve: ReserveData,
    ) {
        let initial_account = UserAccountData {
            total_collateral_base: INITIAL_TOTAL_COLLATERAL_BASE,
            total_debt_base: INITIAL_TOTAL_DEBT_BASE,
            available_borrows_base: 0,
            current_liquidation_threshold: CURRENT_LIQ_THRESHOLD_BPS,
            ltv: 7_500,
            health_factor: INITIAL_HEALTH_FACTOR,
        };
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::UserAccount(user), &initial_account);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::CollateralAsset, &collateral_asset);
        env.storage().instance().set(&RouterKey::DebtAsset, &debt_asset);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::CollateralReserve, &collateral_reserve);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::DebtReserve, &debt_reserve);
    }

    pub fn get_user_account_data(env: Env, user: Address) -> UserAccountData {
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .get(&RouterKey::UserAccount(user))
            .unwrap()
    }

    pub fn get_reserve_data(env: Env, asset: Address) -> ReserveData {
        let collateral_asset: Address = env
            .storage()
            .instance()
            .get(&RouterKey::CollateralAsset)
            .unwrap();
        if asset == collateral_asset {
            env.storage()
                .instance()
                .get(&RouterKey::CollateralReserve)
                .unwrap()
        } else {
            env.storage()
                .instance()
                .get(&RouterKey::DebtReserve)
                .unwrap()
        }
    }

    pub fn liquidation_call(
        env: Env,
        _liquidator: Address,
        _collateral_asset: Address,
        _debt_asset: Address,
        user: Address,
        debt_to_cover: u128,
        _receive_a_token: bool,
    ) {
        let actual = canonical_liquidation(&env, debt_to_cover);

        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::LastCollateralToSeize, &actual.collateral_to_seize);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::LastBonusBase, &actual.bonus_base);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::LastHealthFactor, &actual.health_factor_after);

        let debt_to_cover_base = soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(&env, debt_to_cover)
            .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(&env, DEBT_PRICE))
            .mul(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(
                &env,
                calculate_oracle_to_wad_factor(ORACLE_PRECISION),
            ))
            .div(&soroban_sdk::U256::from_u128(&env, 10u128.pow(DEBT_DECIMALS)))
            .to_u128()
            .unwrap();
        let new_account = UserAccountData {
            total_collateral_base: INITIAL_TOTAL_COLLATERAL_BASE - (debt_to_cover_base + actual.bonus_base),
            total_debt_base: INITIAL_TOTAL_DEBT_BASE - debt_to_cover_base,
            available_borrows_base: 0,
            current_liquidation_threshold: CURRENT_LIQ_THRESHOLD_BPS,
            ltv: 7_500,
            health_factor: actual.health_factor_after,
        };
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&RouterKey::UserAccount(user), &new_account);
    }

    pub fn get_last_collateral_to_seize(env: Env) -> u128 {
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .get(&RouterKey::LastCollateralToSeize)
            .unwrap()
    }

    pub fn get_last_bonus_base(env: Env) -> u128 {
        env.storage().instance().get(&RouterKey::LastBonusBase).unwrap()
    }

    pub fn get_last_health_factor(env: Env) -> u128 {
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .get(&RouterKey::LastHealthFactor)
            .unwrap()
    }
}

#[contracttype]
#[derive(Clone)]
enum OracleKey {
    CollateralAsset,
    DebtAsset,
}

#[contract]
struct MockPriceOracle;

#[contractimpl]
impl MockPriceOracle {
    pub fn initialize(env: Env, collateral_asset: Address, debt_asset: Address) {
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&OracleKey::CollateralAsset, &collateral_asset);
        env.storage()
            .instance()
            .set(&OracleKey::DebtAsset, &debt_asset);
    }

    pub fn get_asset_price(env: Env, asset: Asset) -> Result<u128, KineticRouterError> {
        let requested = match asset {
            Asset::Stellar(addr) => addr,
            Asset::Other(_) => return Err(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound),
        };

        let collateral_asset: Address = env
            .storage()
            .instance()
            .get(&OracleKey::CollateralAsset)
            .unwrap();
        let debt_asset: Address = env.storage().instance().get(&OracleKey::DebtAsset).unwrap();

        if requested == collateral_asset {
            Ok(COLLATERAL_PRICE)
        } else if requested == debt_asset {
            Ok(DEBT_PRICE)
        } else {
            Err(KineticRouterError::PriceOracleNotFound)
        }
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_poc_wrong_unit_liquidation_quote_and_execute_record() {
    let env = Env::default();
    env.mock_all_auths();

    let admin = Address::generate(&env);
    let user = Address::generate(&env);
    let liquidator = Address::generate(&env);
    let collateral_asset = Address::generate(&env);
    let debt_asset = Address::generate(&env);

    let router_id = env.register(MockRouter, ());
    let router = MockRouterClient::new(&env, &router_id);
    router.initialize(
        &user,
        &collateral_asset,
        &debt_asset,
        &reserve_data(&env, 0, COLLATERAL_DECIMALS, LIQUIDATION_BONUS_BPS),
        &reserve_data(&env, 1, DEBT_DECIMALS, LIQUIDATION_BONUS_BPS),
    );

    let oracle_id = env.register(MockPriceOracle, ());
    let oracle = MockPriceOracleClient::new(&env, &oracle_id);
    oracle.initialize(&collateral_asset, &debt_asset);

    let engine_id = env.register(LiquidationEngineContract, ());
    let engine = LiquidationEngineContractClient::new(&env, &engine_id);
    engine.initialize(&admin, &router_id, &oracle_id);

    let quote = engine.calculate_liquidation(&collateral_asset, &debt_asset, &user, &DEBT_TO_COVER);
    let execution = engine.execute_liquidation(
        &liquidator,
        &collateral_asset,
        &debt_asset,
        &user,
        &DEBT_TO_COVER,
        &false,
    );

    let actual = canonical_liquidation(&env, DEBT_TO_COVER);
    let post_account = router.get_user_account_data(&user);

    assert_eq!(router.get_last_collateral_to_seize(), actual.collateral_to_seize);
    assert_eq!(router.get_last_bonus_base(), actual.bonus_base);
    assert_eq!(router.get_last_health_factor(), actual.health_factor_after);

    assert_eq!(quote.collateral_amount, 26_250_000_000);
    assert_eq!(actual.collateral_to_seize, 26_250_000);
    assert_eq!(execution.collateral_to_liquidate, quote.collateral_amount);
    assert_eq!(quote.collateral_amount, actual.collateral_to_seize * 1_000);

    assert_eq!(quote.bonus_amount, 250_000);
    assert_eq!(actual.bonus_base, 2_500_000_000_000_000_000);
    assert_eq!(execution.liquidation_bonus, quote.bonus_amount);
    assert!(actual.bonus_base > execution.liquidation_bonus * 1_000_000_000_000);

    assert_eq!(quote.health_factor_after, 9_600);
    assert_eq!(post_account.health_factor, 1_080_000_000_000_000_000);
    assert_eq!(post_account.health_factor, actual.health_factor_after);

    assert_ne!(execution.collateral_to_liquidate, router.get_last_collateral_to_seize());
    assert_ne!(execution.liquidation_bonus, router.get_last_bonus_base());
    assert_ne!(quote.health_factor_after, post_account.health_factor);
}
```
